ontology.foodex2_description: Broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. italica), raw, florets and stem

main_ingredients: broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. italica), raw florets and tender stems

serving.natural_language_portion: 1 cup of raw broccoli florets weighs approximately 90g. A typical stir-fry portion uses approximately 150–200g raw broccoli.

nutrient_source.title: Broccoli (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F14040036; cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F14040036 — Broccoli, raw.

gi_source: GI not applicable — broccoli has very low digestible starch and minimal glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High vitamin C (89.2mg/100g — exceeds adult daily requirement in a 100g serving), potassium (316mg/100g), and folate (63mcg DFE/100g). Very low sodium (33mg/100g). Broccoli's sulforaphane has demonstrated blood pressure-lowering effects in clinical studies through reducing endothelin-1.
hypertension.guidance: Broccoli is strongly recommended for patients with hypertension. Regular consumption of cruciferous vegetables is associated with reduced cardiovascular risk. Lightly steam rather than boil to preserve vitamin C and glucosinolates.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low calorie (34 kcal/100g), high fibre (2.6g/100g), negligible digestible starch. Sulforaphane has been shown in research to improve insulin sensitivity through Nrf2 pathway activation. Chromium in broccoli supports insulin signalling.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Broccoli is an excellent vegetable for patients with type 2 diabetes. Daily consumption of broccoli is one of the most evidence-backed dietary recommendations for metabolic health. Prefer steaming over boiling which preserves more of the bioactive glucosinolates.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: The sulforaphane in broccoli is a potent inhibitor of NF-κB and MAPK inflammatory signalling pathways in chondrocytes and synoviocytes. Clinical research specifically on sulforaphane and rheumatoid arthritis is promising. Very high vitamin C (89.2mg/100g) for collagen synthesis and antioxidant activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Broccoli is one of the most specifically recommended vegetables for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. Sulforaphane's direct anti-inflammatory mechanism in joint tissue is clinically relevant. Aim for 100–200g broccoli 3–4 times per week. Lightly steamed is the optimal preparation for sulforaphane availability.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. italica) is a cruciferous vegetable with dark green flowering heads (florets) and thick edible stems. Nutritionally outstanding: 89.2mg vitamin C per 100g raw (more than an orange), 63mcg DFE folate, 316mg potassium, 2.8g protein, and 2.6g dietary fibre. The bioactive compound sulforaphane (formed from glucoraphanin + myrosinase enzyme activation when cell walls are broken) is one of the most studied dietary anti-inflammatory and anti-cancer compounds. Sulforaphane is optimally produced by chewing raw broccoli or lightly steaming (not boiling or microwaving for extended periods). Used in Singapore in oyster sauce broccoli stir-fry, broccoli soup, salads, and as a side vegetable in Western-style plates.

confidence_reason: Exact match in HPB Singapore Foodo Database (F14040036).
notes: Values per 100g raw broccoli florets and tender stem. Sulforaphane content is maximised by chewing raw broccoli or steaming lightly (3–5 minutes) — myrosinase enzyme is destroyed by boiling and extended microwaving, reducing sulforaphane production. Vitamin C is highly heat-sensitive — raw broccoli has 89.2mg/100g vs approximately 50–65mg/100g lightly steamed.
